questions.stem: An isomorphism from a group $G$ to a group $H$ is a function $\phi : G \to H$ that is which of the following?
options: A bijection, with no condition on the operation.
A bijection that preserves the operation: $\phi(ab) = \phi(a)\phi(b)$.
questions.explanation: An isomorphism is a bijective homomorphism: it is one-to-one and onto, and it preserves the operation, $\phi(ab) = \phi(a)\phi(b)$. Dropping bijectivity gives a mere homomorphism; dropping the operation condition gives a mere bijection.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: When two groups $G$ and $H$ are isomorphic, written $G \cong H$, what does this signify?
options: They are literally the same set.
One is a subgroup of the other.
They have the same number of elements only.
They have the same algebraic structure (are essentially identical as groups).
questions.explanation: Isomorphic groups have identical group structure: a relabeling of elements via the isomorphism turns one Cayley table into the other. They need not be the same set, and same cardinality alone does not imply isomorphism.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: If $\phi : G \to H$ is an isomorphism and $e_G$ is the identity of $G$, what is $\phi(e_G)$?
questions.explanation: Every homomorphism, and so every isomorphism, sends the identity to the identity: $\phi(e_G) = e_H$. From $\phi(e_G) = \phi(e_G e_G) = \phi(e_G)\phi(e_G)$, cancellation in $H$ gives $\phi(e_G) = e_H$.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: If $\phi : G \to H$ is an isomorphism and $a \in G$ has order $n$, what is the order of $\phi(a)$?
questions.explanation: Isomorphisms preserve the order of elements: $|\phi(a)| = |a| = n$. Since $\phi(a)^k = \phi(a^k)$ and $\phi$ is injective, $\phi(a)^k = e_H$ holds exactly when $a^k = e_G$, so the least such $k$ is the same.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: Which property is preserved by an isomorphism between groups?
options: The font used to write the operation.
The particular names of the elements.
questions.explanation: Isomorphisms preserve all structural (group-theoretic) properties, including being abelian: if $G$ is abelian and $G \cong H$, then $H$ is abelian too, because $\phi(a)\phi(b) = \phi(ab) = \phi(ba) = \phi(b)\phi(a)$. Element names and external descriptions are not structural.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: A necessary condition for two finite groups to be isomorphic is that they have which feature in common?
questions.explanation: Isomorphic groups must have the same order, since an isomorphism is in particular a bijection. This is necessary but not sufficient: $\mathbb{Z}_4$ and the Klein four-group both have order $4$ yet are not isomorphic.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: Are the groups $\mathbb{Z}_4$ and the Klein four-group $\mathbb{Z}_2 \times \mathbb{Z}_2$ isomorphic?
options: No, $\mathbb{Z}_4$ has an element of order $4$ but the Klein four-group does not.
questions.explanation: Both have order $4$ and are abelian, but they differ structurally: $\mathbb{Z}_4$ is cyclic with an element of order $4$, whereas every non-identity element of $\mathbb{Z}_2 \times \mathbb{Z}_2$ has order $2$. Order of elements is an isomorphism invariant, so they are not isomorphic.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: Which is the most efficient way to prove two finite groups are NOT isomorphic?
options: Check that every possible bijection fails to preserve the operation.
Show their element sets are different.
Exhibit a structural invariant on which they differ, such as the number of elements of some order.
Verify they have the same order.
questions.explanation: To disprove isomorphism, find a structural invariant where the groups disagree: order, abelian or not, cyclic or not, or the count of elements of a given order. Any single mismatch suffices. Checking all bijections is correct in principle but hopelessly inefficient.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: An isomorphism from a group $G$ to itself is called what?
questions.explanation: An isomorphism of a group onto itself is an automorphism. The set of all automorphisms of $G$, denoted $\text{Aut}(G)$, forms a group under composition.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: The set $\text{Aut}(G)$ of all automorphisms of $G$ forms a group under which operation?
questions.explanation: $\text{Aut}(G)$ is a group under composition of functions: the composite of two automorphisms is an automorphism, the identity map is the identity element, and each automorphism has an inverse that is also an automorphism.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: For the cyclic group $\mathbb{Z}_n$, the automorphism group $\text{Aut}(\mathbb{Z}_n)$ is isomorphic to which group?
questions.explanation: An automorphism of $\mathbb{Z}_n$ is determined by where it sends the generator $1$, and it must go to another generator, i.e. a unit mod $n$. This gives $\text{Aut}(\mathbb{Z}_n) \cong U(n)$, a group of order $\phi(n)$.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: What is the order of the automorphism group $\text{Aut}(\mathbb{Z}_{10})$?
questions.explanation: $\text{Aut}(\mathbb{Z}_{10}) \cong U(10)$, which has order $\phi(10) = 4$. The four automorphisms send the generator $1$ to one of the four generators $1, 3, 7, 9$ of $\mathbb{Z}_{10}$.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: Cayley's theorem states that every group $G$ is isomorphic to which of the following?
options: A subgroup of some symmetric group.
questions.explanation: Cayley's theorem says every group is isomorphic to a subgroup of a symmetric group (specifically the group of all permutations of $G$). The embedding sends each $g$ to the permutation $x \mapsto gx$ of $G$. This shows permutation groups are universal.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: By Cayley's theorem, a finite group of order $n$ embeds as a subgroup of which symmetric group?
questions.explanation: A group of order $n$ is isomorphic to a subgroup of $S_n$, because left multiplication realizes each element as a permutation of the $n$ group elements. So every order-$n$ group lives inside $S_n$, whose order is $n!$.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: Is the group $U(10)$ isomorphic to $\mathbb{Z}_4$?
questions.explanation: $U(10) = \{1, 3, 7, 9\}$ is cyclic of order $4$, generated by $3$ (since $3, 9, 7, 1$ are its powers). A cyclic group of order $4$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb{Z}_4$, so $U(10) \cong \mathbb{Z}_4$.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: Is the group $U(8)$ isomorphic to $\mathbb{Z}_4$ or to the Klein four-group $\mathbb{Z}_2 \times \mathbb{Z}_2$?
options: To both, since they are isomorphic to each other.
To the Klein four-group, since every non-identity element has order $2$.
questions.explanation: In $U(8) = \{1, 3, 5, 7\}$ every non-identity element squares to $1$, so all have order $2$. That matches the Klein four-group, not the cyclic $\mathbb{Z}_4$. Hence $U(8) \cong \mathbb{Z}_2 \times \mathbb{Z}_2$.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: The relation 'is isomorphic to' on the class of all groups is which kind of relation?
questions.explanation: Isomorphism is an equivalence relation: it is reflexive (the identity map), symmetric (the inverse of an isomorphism is an isomorphism), and transitive (composition of isomorphisms is an isomorphism). This lets us speak of groups up to isomorphism.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: Under an isomorphism $\phi : G \to H$ with $G$ cyclic, the image $H$ must be which of the following?
questions.explanation: Being cyclic is preserved by isomorphism: if $G = \langle a \rangle$ then $H = \phi(G) = \langle \phi(a) \rangle$, so $H$ is cyclic generated by $\phi(a)$. Isomorphisms carry generators to generators.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: Are the additive groups $\mathbb{Z}$ and $\mathbb{Z}_6$ isomorphic?
options: No, one is infinite and the other is finite.
questions.explanation: An isomorphism is a bijection, so isomorphic groups have the same cardinality. $\mathbb{Z}$ is infinite while $\mathbb{Z}_6$ has six elements, so no bijection exists between them and they cannot be isomorphic, even though both are cyclic.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: The map $\phi : \mathbb{Z}_6 \to \mathbb{Z}_2 \times \mathbb{Z}_3$ given by $\phi(k) = (k \bmod 2,\ k \bmod 3)$ is an isomorphism. What does this illustrate?
options: That all groups of order $6$ are isomorphic.
questions.explanation: Because $\gcd(2,3) = 1$, the Chinese remainder map is a bijective homomorphism, giving $\mathbb{Z}_6 \cong \mathbb{Z}_2 \times \mathbb{Z}_3$. In general $\mathbb{Z}_m \times \mathbb{Z}_n \cong \mathbb{Z}_{mn}$ exactly when $m$ and $n$ are coprime.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: If $\phi : G \to H$ is an isomorphism, what is the inverse map $\phi^{-1} : H \to G$?
questions.explanation: The inverse of an isomorphism is again an isomorphism. It is automatically a bijection, and it preserves the operation: applying $\phi$ to both sides of $\phi^{-1}(xy) = \phi^{-1}(x)\phi^{-1}(y)$ confirms the identity. This symmetry is why $G \cong H$ and $H \cong G$ mean the same thing.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: Two cyclic groups are isomorphic if and only if which condition holds?
options: They have the same order (or are both infinite).
questions.explanation: Cyclic groups are classified by size: two cyclic groups are isomorphic exactly when they have the same order, or are both infinite. So any cyclic group of order $n$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb{Z}_n$, and any infinite cyclic group to $\mathbb{Z}$.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: How many elements of order $2$ does the Klein four-group $\mathbb{Z}_2 \times \mathbb{Z}_2$ have, and how does this distinguish it from $\mathbb{Z}_4$?
questions.explanation: In $\mathbb{Z}_2 \times \mathbb{Z}_2$ the three non-identity elements $(1,0), (0,1), (1,1)$ all have order $2$. In $\mathbb{Z}_4$ only the element $2$ has order $2$. The differing counts of order-$2$ elements prove the two groups are not isomorphic.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: Suppose $G$ is a group of order $5$. Up to isomorphism, what must $G$ be?
questions.explanation: A group of prime order $p$ is cyclic, hence isomorphic to $\mathbb{Z}_p$. With $p = 5$, the only group of order $5$ up to isomorphism is $\mathbb{Z}_5$; there is no non-abelian option.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: Consider the groups $\mathbb{Z}_4$ and the Klein four-group $\mathbb{Z}_2 \times \mathbb{Z}_2$. Both have the same order; state that common order, and decide whether the two groups are isomorphic.
questions.solution: Each group has order {{1}}. However, $\mathbb{Z}_4$ has an element of order $4$ while the Klein four-group does not, so the two groups are {{2}}.
questions.explanation: Both groups have order $4$. The presence of an order-$4$ element in $\mathbb{Z}_4$ but not in $\mathbb{Z}_2 \times \mathbb{Z}_2$ is a structural difference, so they are not isomorphic.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: By Cayley's theorem, every finite group embeds in a symmetric group. For a group of order $6$, give the smallest $n$ guaranteed by the standard construction so that the group is isomorphic to a subgroup of $S_n$, and give $|S_n|$ for that $n$.
questions.solution: The standard Cayley embedding uses left multiplication on the $6$ group elements, so it realizes the group inside $S_n$ with $n = $ {{1}}. The order of that symmetric group is $n!$, which equals {{2}}.
questions.explanation: Cayley's theorem embeds a group of order $n$ into $S_n$; here $n = 6$. Then $|S_6| = 6! = 720$. The actual group of order $6$ may sit in a smaller symmetric group, but the standard construction guarantees $S_6$.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: Use the fact that $\text{Aut}(\mathbb{Z}_n) \cong U(n)$. Give the order of $\text{Aut}(\mathbb{Z}_8)$, and state whether $\text{Aut}(\mathbb{Z}_8)$ is cyclic.
questions.solution: The order of $\text{Aut}(\mathbb{Z}_8)$ equals $|U(8)| = \phi(8)$, which is {{1}}. Since $U(8)$ is the Klein four-group (every non-identity element has order $2$), $\text{Aut}(\mathbb{Z}_8)$ is {{2}}.
questions.explanation: $|\text{Aut}(\mathbb{Z}_8)| = |U(8)| = \phi(8) = 4$. Because $U(8) \cong \mathbb{Z}_2 \times \mathbb{Z}_2$ has no element of order $4$, it is not cyclic, so neither is $\text{Aut}(\mathbb{Z}_8)$.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: Consider $\mathbb{Z}_6$ and the product $\mathbb{Z}_2 \times \mathbb{Z}_3$. Give the order of each group, and state whether they are isomorphic.
questions.solution: Both $\mathbb{Z}_6$ and $\mathbb{Z}_2 \times \mathbb{Z}_3$ have order {{1}}. Because $\gcd(2,3) = 1$, the product is cyclic, so the two groups are {{2}}.
questions.explanation: Both groups have order $6$. Since $2$ and $3$ are coprime, $\mathbb{Z}_2 \times \mathbb{Z}_3 \cong \mathbb{Z}_6$ by the Chinese remainder theorem, so the groups are isomorphic.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra
